steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The study’s key finding: musicians and nonmusicians have equal sound-detection ability, yet musicians chose higher volumes to reach maximum pleasure. Dolan et al. conclude something about why — specifically, how musicians’ brain alterations affect the hedonic (pleasure) response to volume.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — previous research may have overstated the effects of the physical differences between musicians' brains and nonmusicians' brains.: the study found a real behavioral difference (volume preference), which suggests the brain differences matter — not that they were overstated. ✗
  • Choice B — physical aspects of musicians' brains may suppress the hedonic response to music at volumes that provoke the hedonic response in nonmusicians.: if musicians’ brains require more volume to reach the same pleasure peak, then at lower volumes their hedonic response is suppressed — exactly what the preference data shows. ✓
  • Choice C — characteristics of musicians' brain anatomy that are not shared with nonmusicians result in musicians experiencing the hedonic response due to characteristics of music other than its volume.: the study is specifically about volume preference, and this statement shifts the hedonic trigger away from volume entirely, contradicting the study’s design. ✗
  • Choice D — musical training may alter musicians' brains such that they have a reduced ability to detect sounds that are unlikely to incite the hedonic response.: the passage explicitly states musicians and nonmusicians were clinically comparable in sound-detection ability, ruling this out. ✗
B is correct.
